titleEn: I Protected My Soul from Defilement

verses.ajuz: أن أرى غير مصبح حيث أمسي

verses.sadr: غير أني أراه يشهد أن لم يكن

translationEn: The poet declares he has kept his soul pure from all defilement, lifting himself above groveling to the base. He reflects bitterly on the vicissitudes of fate and the exchange of Syria's warmth for Iraq's hardship. He travels to al-Mada'in, the ancient capital of the Sassanids, meditating on the ruins of their glorious civilization. The ode ends as a tribute to those who once supported the caliphate with courage and valor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I held myself together, restrained myself
a bad deal, a loss in trade

context: Al-Buhturi composed this poem during a period of personal hardship, lamenting the decline of his fortunes and his move from Syria to Iraq. The meditation on the Sassanid ruins at al-Mada'in is characteristic of al-Buhturi's wasf poetry, where he combines personal complaint with historical elegy.

titleEn: Youth's Garment and Old Age's Acceptance

verses.sadr: وإذا ما امتعضت من ولع الشيب

verses.ajuz: فيه ، إلا عن غفلة أو تغاض

verses.sadr: غير نفع إلا التعلل من شخص
طبت نفسا عن الشباب وما سود
يكثر الحظ في أناس وإن قل
جامح في العنان لا يسمع الزجر ،
زاعم أن طيف بدعة قد أندب
أجلبوا تحت غابة من قنا الخط ،
ليس من عصبة ، إذا استأنفوا السعي
بأبي أنت ، أنت أول من حولني
ما الندى في سواك غير حديث

translationEn: The poet opens with a meditation on youth and old age, wondering whether to wear the garment of youth or resign himself to white hair. He reflects bitterly on time, rivals, and the foolishness of Ibn Ayyub who recklessly brought troops against him. The ode pivots to praise of a patron whose generosity saved poetry itself from extinction, comparing his beneficence to spring rains that reveal hidden blossoms.
context: This poem blends personal elegy on aging with sharp satire of a rival before concluding with praise of a generous patron. Al-Buhturi's characteristic shift from intimate self-reflection to panegyric is on full display here.

titleEn: An Apology to Abu Ja'far

verses.sadr: وإذا ما أردت أن تمنع الناس
إنما كنت واردا في جميع الناس

translationEn: The poet addresses Abu Ja'far with an apology, noting that news of their friendship has spread through Manbij. He asks forgiveness for his long-standing offenses and describes Ibn al-Ghala'ili's company as gifting him beauty, song, and poetry alike. He concludes with the flattering comparison that trying to keep the people from the Euphrates would be as futile as denying his patron's generosity.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: traps, nets; or gathering places near water
one who grants access, who lets flow

context: A short poem of apology and affection addressed to Abu Ja'far, blending the erotic with the social. Al-Buhturi's light touch here reflects his mastery of the khafif meter for shorter, more personal compositions.

titleEn: My Only Condition Is Fairness
translationEn: The poet declares his only stipulation in friendship is fairness; he asks nothing beyond justice from other men. He addresses a nobleman of sound judgment, accusing someone of excess in their demands. He closes with a resigned philosophical observation: life's vicissitudes are like a star that rises then falls — he cannot be diminished by fate's arithmetic.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: grey hair mixed with black, old age

context: A brief reflective poem in the ramal meter on the poet's philosophy of fair dealing. Al-Buhturi presents himself as a man of principle who asks only for justice, not favor.

titleEn: Your Counsel O Abu Nahshal
translationEn: The poet humorously addresses Abu Nahshal, asking why he would desire the company of a crude man from Khuttal whose boorish behavior would harm fellow drinkers. The poem is a sharp lampoon against a rival poet Abu al-Barq, predicting his verses will be devoured and forgotten. The imagery is deliberately coarse and satirical.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you drink with him as a companion
the grey-haired man with a receding hairline

context: A satirical poem (hija') aimed at a rival poet, using the mutaqarib meter and deliberately crude imagery to demean the target. Al-Buhturi's satires, though less celebrated than his panegyrics, show considerable wit.

titleAr: ترى الليل يقضي عقبة من هزيعه
titleEn: Does the Night Still Have Hours Remaining

verses.sadr: ترى الليل يقضي عقبة من هزيعه ،
verses.ajuz: أم الصبح يجلو غرة من صديعه
على وجده ، أو زائد في ولوعه

verses.sadr: ولم تبن دار العجز للملحس الذي
وليس امرأ إلا امرؤ ذهبت به
وكان اختيال العلج من عطش الردى

verses.ajuz: وقد كان يكفي بعضه من جميعه

verses.sadr: ولم ينقلب من بست ، إلا ورأيه
فإن يحي لا يفلح ، وإن يثو لا يكن
دم إن يرق لا يقض تبلا مراقه ،
شفى برح الأكباد أن ابن طاهر

verses.ajuz: ولا يخف كافي شانها من مضيعه

verses.sadr: متى قظت في شرق البلاد ، فإنني
ومرضى من الحساد قد كان شفهم
وما عذرهم في أن تعل صدورهم
فلا عجب أن يطلب السيل نهجه ،

translationEn: The poem opens with lyrical reflections on the night, longing, and sleeplessness born of passionate love. The poet philosophizes on old age and the folly of rivals. The ode then praises Ibn Tahir and celebrates the hope that his leadership will bring light to Khorasan as a full moon rises from the west, routing enemies and silencing envious detractors.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a coppersmith; here a derogatory epithet

context: A long qasida that moves from amatory opening (nasib) through self-reflection and satire of a rival, then to praise of Ibn Tahir as a leader destined to illuminate Khorasan. The poem exemplifies the classical qasida structure.

titleEn: He Burdened Me Beyond My Capacity

verses.ajuz: في الرأي ، أن يأمر من لا يطيع

verses.sadr: طول هذا الليل أن لا كرى
verses.ajuz: يريك من تهوى وأن لا هجوع
فما كث عن حظه ، أو سريع

translationEn: The poet opens with complaints about a blamer who burdens him beyond capacity and commands him to forget his beloved Asma'. He philosophizes on patience, contentment, and the two kinds of wealth — giving and withholding. The ode then turns to lavish praise of Abu Ghanim, a patron of towering stature and terrifying power, whose generosity is as sweet as fresh musk and whose protection has been the poet's refuge throughout his life.
context: This lengthy qasida in the sari' meter follows the classical form: amatory prelude (nasib) lamenting Asma', followed by moral reflections, then an extended panegyric to Abu Ghanim. Al-Buhturi's description of the patron on horseback is among his finest descriptive passages.

titleEn: We Commend the Departing Traveler to God

verses.sadr: بنو مخلد إن يشرع الحمد يشرهوا
verses.ajuz: إليه ، وإن يدعوا إلى المجد يسرعوا

verses.sadr: إذا نحن شيعنا من القوم واحدا ،

translationEn: The poet reflects on the farewell of a departing traveler, whom they commend to God's protection. He praises Banu Mukhalid: when praise is initiated they rush toward it, and when called to glory they hasten. So strong is their bond of loyalty that when they escort one of their own away, they forsake sleep until he returns.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a departing traveler, one setting out

context: A brief three-verse fragment praising Banu Mukhalid for their fierce loyalty and eagerness to pursue glory. The poem is a concise jewel of panegyric in the tawil meter.

titleEn: The Brotherhood of Necessity Has Departed

verses.ajuz: بك القول شأوا رد منك فأسرعا

verses.sadr: وما أنا بالظمآن فيك إلى التي
أغار على ما بيننا أن يناله
verses.ajuz: لسان عدو لم يجد فيك مطمعا

verses.sadr: وكم حفرة في غور نجران أشفقت
ملكت عنان الهجر أن يبلغ المدى ،
فإن تدعني للشر أسرع ، وإن تهب

translationEn: The poet addresses an estranged companion whose friendship was born of necessity; that bond is now fraying. He says he started it but the wrong was plain, and his words drove the friend further away. Rather than letting enemies feast on their quarrel or unleashing angry verses, he held back the reins of estrangement. He ends with an ultimatum: if called to hostility he will rush there, but he has left room for reconciliation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a friend of circumstance, not sincere brotherhood
I disdain, I find beneath dignity

context: A poem of complaint and guarded reconciliation addressed to an estranged friend. The poet balances wounded pride with a desire to preserve the relationship, revealing al-Buhturi's skill in navigating personal conflicts through verse.

titleEn: Al-Kumaiti the Elder Abandoned Us
translationEn: The elder Kumaiti abandoned the poet, and the younger Kumaiti offered no intercession. Neither brought comfort during their stay, even knowing that departure was imminent. The poet asks bitterly: if they severed the bond while living side by side, how could they ever reconnect when scattered apart?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: he abandoned us, turned away from us
an intercessor, one who pleads on another's behalf
separation is swift, departure comes quickly

context: A compact three-verse lament about two companions named al-Kumaiti — elder and younger — who abandoned the poet despite proximity. The poem's rhetorical power lies in its final paradox about distance and closeness.

titleEn: A Master Who Imposed Humiliation on Me

verses.sadr: إن كنت لا تنوي نجاحا لها ،
هل لك في الصلح ، فأعفيك من

translationEn: The poet addresses a patron who has imposed humiliation and broken a promise of a hundred dinars, repeatedly deferring payment. He invokes the faithfulness of Isma'il and Ibrahim as examples the patron has failed to match. He proposes a compromise: forgive half and pay the rest, or at least restore the friendship on equal footing — for a burden too heavy deserves to be exchanged for something lighter.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: imposed humiliation and injustice on me
taking his time, being slow about it

context: A frank poem of complaint about an unfulfilled monetary promise, blending indignation with humor and a pragmatic proposal for settlement. It reveals the real-world patronage pressures al-Buhturi navigated throughout his career.

titleEn: The Phantom of Mawiyya Visits Me
translationEn: The poet is kept awake by the phantom of Mawiyya, a beautiful woman with a weak waist weighed down by the dunes behind her. He shifts to praise of Abdun, a unique individual of the al-Harith ibn Ka'b tribe whose generosity is so great that notables of every people cluster at his gate. The poem ends with a charming moment of equality: the poet and the governor of al-Sawad were alike in that night, both guests of the same refined host.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the one who circles, the phantom that visits

context: A poem in the munsarih meter that opens with an erotic description of Mawiyya, pivots to praise of the generous Abdun, and ends with a memorable vignette of the poet dining beside the governor — both equally guests of this magnanimous host.

titleEn: Greetings to the Summer and Spring Abodes

verses.sadr: تهتز في هيف ، وما بعث الهوى
إذ لا ينهنهني العذول ولا أرى
صنع الصنائع في الرجال ، ولم يكن

translationEn: The poem opens with an address to the spring and summer dwellings of Zaynab and Saduf, the beloved women whose memory keeps the poet awake. He describes their slender figures with vivid imagery before resolving to end his lovesickness and turn to gratitude. He praises Abu Ali whose largesse established both his old and new wealth, and whose handsome son Wasif is like the full moon when he faces the poet's needs. He closes with a petition, confident that one good deed will be followed by another.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: shed tears, tears that pour down
a nobleman of fine stock, generous

context: A full classical qasida moving from nasib (amatory prelude at abandoned dwellings) through self-exhortation to abandon love, culminating in elaborate praise of Abu Ali and his luminous son Wasif. The poem closes with a refined petition for continued patronage.

titleEn: Hadramaut and Its Distant Tribes

verses.sadr: نحن من قد علمت في الشرف الوافي ،

translationEn: The poet addresses a rival with cutting irony, questioning whether he lampoons the poet's father or his own Khath'ami cobbler ancestor. He asserts his own tribe's noble lineage far surpasses any claim the rival might make and suggests that scrutinizing the rival's patriarch would tax even a money-changer's patience.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a cobbler, shoemaker (used as an insult)
a money-changer, one who scrutinizes coins

context: A brief poem of tribal boasting (fakhr) and counter-satire, asserting the poet's lineage against a rival's pretensions. The reference to Hadramaut's distance and the cobbler ancestor are classic devices of poetic invective.

titleEn: A Swaying Beauty Far in Affection

verses.sadr: إذا بعدت أبلت وإن قربت شفت ،
بذلت لها الود الذي بخلت به ،
verses.ajuz: وأصفيتها الود الذي لم تكن تصفي
وإن الذي أبدي لدون الذي أخفي

verses.sadr: أما يطمع المحروم عندك في الجدا ،
verses.ajuz: ولا يطمع المظلوم عندك في النصف
فأكرم به من ردء قوم ومن كهف
فلا ناقص الجدوى ولا جامد الكف
كما حن إلف مستهام إلى إلف

verses.sadr: ويقلق حتى ينجز الوعد مثل ما
verses.ajuz: يجافي الذي يمشي على رمض الرضف
من النيل ، أصبح في أمان من الخلف

verses.sadr: وما ألف ألف من جداك كثيرة ،
verses.ajuz: وكيف أخاف الفوت عندك في ألف

translationEn: The poem opens with the description of a swaying beauty — delicate of limb, languid of gaze — whose distance sickens and whose nearness heals. The poet confesses he gave her more love than she returned, and all he revealed was less than what he concealed. He pivots to praise of the Caliph, the shield of the Muslims, who longs to do good as ardently as a lovesick man longs for his companion. He ends with a confident plea: a thousand thousand of the Caliph's gifts is not too many, and surely a mere thousand is safe.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: swaying at the sides, gracefully moving
the refuge, cave of protection for Muslims

context: A qasida in the tawil meter structured classically: an erotic description of an unnamed beloved leads into praise of the Caliph as a man who yearns to be generous as intensely as a lover yearns for union. The closing petition for a thousand dinars is characteristic of al-Buhturi's frank patronage poetry.

titleEn: By the One Who Gave You Virtue and Power

verses.ajuz: على كل حي ، واصطفاك على الخلق
سنا الشمس من أفق ووجهك من أفق
سلكت بها نهج السبيل إلى الحق

translationEn: The poet swears by God who gave the Caliph virtue over all creation that his rule is one of justice, generosity, and gentleness. He sees the Prophet's mark in the Caliph's bright, open face and praises how he saved Homs and its people through clemency rather than the sword. His appearance before them was like two suns rising at once. The poem closes with the memorable image: the loyalty of those freed from destruction surpasses even the loyalty of those freed from slavery.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: they committed, they were guilty of

context: A panegyric to the Caliph al-Mutawakkil praising his pacification of Homs through clemency. The theological opening and comparison to the Prophet's radiance reflect al-Buhturi's ability to elevate political praise to a sacred register.

titleEn: I Said to the Blamer at the Well: Wake Up

verses.ajuz: لا تهون طعم شيء لم تذق

verses.sadr: لا يلذ الملتقى ، إن لم يكن

verses.ajuz: لم يقل إن المنايا في الحدق

verses.sadr: إن تكن محتسبا من قد ثوى
ليس فيه غير ما يغري به ،

verses.ajuz: من قبيح في رهان ، أو سبق

verses.sadr: علم في الإفك ، لو قال لنا

verses.ajuz: قد رعى في مسرح الذم وزق

verses.sadr: لو صفرنا عب في الماء ، ولو
إن مشى هملج ، أو صاح إلى
لا وظيف العير مرقوم ولا العجب

verses.ajuz: يتبرأ من عشى ، أو من سرق
أن أرى في أعين الحمر زرق
من لم يك في قعر النفق
فضل ما أوبق ميتا من غرق

verses.sadr: لو توفي ما لنا في صاعد ،

verses.ajuz: لا يرعك الحظ لم يؤخذ بحق
غصن إن لم يكن غض الورق

verses.sadr: لا يحب الخرق ، إلا في الوغى ،

translationEn: The poet opens by rebuking a blamer, philosophizing on sleeplessness, longing, and love. He then launches a ferocious satire against a contemptible rival — describing his baseness, blue eyes, unknown lineage, and worthless friendship in a cascade of insults. The qasida then pivots dramatically to praise of the Mukhladi family, elevating Sai'd as a generous, decisive, warrior-statesman of the Madhij tribe whose renown far outstrips his formal rank.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the well; also: the cistern of the heart
the guest of slumber, sleep's visitor
a spear from Khatt (famous for spear-making)
last breath of life, a thread of life

context: One of al-Buhturi's longest poems in this batch, combining satire and panegyric in the ramal meter. The savagely detailed portrait of the rival and the heroic portrait of the patron represent two poles of the classical Arabic qasida tradition at their most elaborate.

titleAr: أن رق لي قلبك مما ألاق
titleEn: If Your Heart Softened for My Suffering

verses.sadr: أن رق لي قلبك مما ألاق
من لم يبحك النصح من قلبه ،
verses.ajuz: فما له في دينه من خلاق

translationEn: The poet begs his beloved to soften her heart and grant a farewell kiss before departure, confessing he fears separation because of the pain but craves it for the embrace it requires. He pivots to praise of the Caliph Ja'far, whose obedience is a religious duty and whose survival ensures that good persists in the world. He then hymns Damascus as a paradise — its breezes fresh, its water sweet, its seasons fair — and urges the Caliph to prefer it over Iraq.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one deeply in debt, one enslaved by love
sweet water of the lightning-lit meadow

context: A poem that elegantly weaves amatory opening with political panegyric, praising the Caliph al-Mutawakkil (Ja'far) and advocating for Damascus. The description of Damascus as a paradise rivals al-Buhturi's celebrated descriptions of natural landscapes.

titleEn: Your Hands Drew Near with Noble Deeds

verses.sadr: ما كان صوب المزن يطمع قبلها
verses.ajuz: في أن يجيء نداه ، قبل نداكا
من طيب عرفك ، أو جميل ثناكا

translationEn: The poet praises Abu Nuh whose generous hands have drawn near to all who seek, comparing even spring to his brother because both bring abundance. He describes a gathering of young men waiting for the morning drink, whose wine seems to carry the scent of the patron's good repute. The cups pass and stories of his unique virtues replace the need for song entirely.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: your reach, your extent of giving

context: A refined symposium poem (khamriyya-tinged madih) for Abu Nuh, linking the pleasure of wine, the beauty of spring, and the patron's generosity into a single extended metaphor. The intimate drinking-session setting is characteristic of al-Buhturi's social poetry.

titleEn: Will You Hear the One Who Calls to You

verses.sadr: يا يوسف بن محمد دعوى امرىء ،
ما زلت مذ جاريت سابق معشر
صرفوك عن حرب الثغور بقدر ما
والروم تعلم أن سيفك لم يزل
أرض تتيه على السحاب إذا التقى
لم ترو دجلة ظمأة مني ، وقد

translationEn: The poet calls to Yusuf ibn Muhammad, a man whose giving hands and bright face never disappoint those who seek them out. He praises Yusuf's warlike reputation against the Byzantines, whose kings have fallen to his sword, and suggests the sea itself could barely contain his reach. The poet confesses that since parting from this patron, he has been in poverty and forced to praise liars — Yusuf's generosity is the only true sustenance.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Ceyhan river of Anatolia (frontier region)
drawing up, pulling from a well
liars, deceivers (whom one is forced to praise)

context: A praise poem for Yusuf ibn Muhammad, a frontier commander, contrasting the glorious past with the poet's current hardship. The river Seyhan places the addressee on the Byzantine frontier, linking personal patronage to the defense of Islam.

titleEn: We Offer Ourselves for You: Time Is Relentless

verses.ajuz: فمن منزل رحب ومن منزل ضنك

verses.sadr: وما أنت بالمهزوز جأشا على الأذى ،
على أنه قد ضيم في حبسك الهدى ،
verses.ajuz: وأضحى بك الإسلام في قبضة الشرك

verses.sadr: أما في نبي الله يوسف أسوة
أقام جميل الصبر في السجن برهة ،
verses.ajuz: فآل به الصبر الجميل إلى الملك

translationEn: The poet offers himself as a ransom for a patron who suffers in prison. He consoles him: time alternates between spacious and narrow stations, and adversity refines a man as smelting purifies pure gold. He draws a famous parallel — the Prophet Joseph, imprisoned unjustly, endured with beautiful patience, and that patience ultimately led him to kingship. The same destiny awaits this nobly suffering prisoner.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may we be your ransom, we sacrifice ourselves for you
one shaken in courage, easily frightened
a period of time, a while

context: A poem of consolation for a patron unjustly imprisoned, using the Quranic story of the Prophet Joseph as the central consoling image. This combination of classical allusion with personal sympathy shows al-Buhturi's range beyond pure panegyric.

titleAr: لو يكون الحباء حسب الذي أنت
titleEn: Were Gifts Commensurate to Your Worth

verses.sadr: لو يكون الحباء حسب الذي أنت

translationEn: The poet says that if gifts were proportional to what the addressee truly deserves, he would be showered with silver, pearls, and rubies scattered by the handful — and still that would fall short. He closes with a gracious note: a noble, refined person accepts the apology of a friend of modest means.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a person of little means, the poor

context: A brief three-verse gem of apology and flattery, gracefully excusing a modest gift by exalting the recipient's worth beyond any material measure. Al-Buhturi's economy of expression here is remarkable.

titleEn: May My Father Be Your Ransom

verses.sadr: غير أني رددت برك ، إذ كان

translationEn: The poet ransoms his father for his patron, declaring him worthy of all piety and noble endeavor. He notes that a small gift grows large in the eyes of one who hopes for it, while a large gift can seem small. He playfully refuses a returned favor, saying it would constitute forbidden usury. He ends with a witty point: if poetry is repaid with poetry of equal worth, any money given is surplus.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may my father be your ransom (expression of devotion)
the one who hopes in you, your seeker
usury, interest on a loan (forbidden in Islam)
the gold coins are surplus, extra beyond what is owed

context: A charming four-verse piece playing on the economics of poetic exchange and gift-giving. The humor about usury and the equation of equal verse with fair payment gives this short poem a pleasingly intellectual wit.

titleEn: Tell the Cloud Driven by the North Wind

verses.sadr: لا تكذبن ، فأنت ألطف في الحشا
لو شئت عدت إلى التناصب في الهوى ،
إن الرعية لم تزل في سيرة
هي أفضل الرتب ، التي جعلت له

verses.ajuz: في ظل ملك ادركوا ما أملوا

verses.sadr: قد نافس الغيب الحضور على الذي

translationEn: The poem opens with the classical cloud-messenger motif: the poet tells the north-wind-driven cloud to stop at Aleppo and greet his beloved Alawwa. He confesses his contradictory love — he approaches while she retreats, he gives love freely while she withholds. The ode pivots to praise of the Caliph al-Mutawakkil as the inheritor of Umaric governance, a forgiving king who awed even the Byzantine envoys into speechless reverence at his banquet. Their hands trembled with astonishment as they tried to eat, overwhelmed by the Caliph's glory.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the banquet table, the spread of food
confused, bewildered (people of Dhuhl tribe; here: astonished)

context: One of al-Buhturi's major odes praising al-Mutawakkil, famous for its vivid account of the Byzantine delegation's astonishment at the Caliph's court. The cloud-messenger device (risala) opening is a literary sophistication that elevates the poem beyond standard panegyric.

titleEn: Were You Not to Rebuke Me, I Would Say

verses.ajuz: حرقا توقد في الحشا ، ما ترحل
نشوان يجمل فيه ما لا يجمل

verses.sadr: يا دار ! لا زالت رباك مجودة ،
وسألت من لا يستجيب فكنت في

verses.ajuz: من قبل أن يقع القضاء ، فتعقل

verses.sadr: لم تنصرف عنها النفوس ولم تزغ
أوما ترى حسن الزمان ، وما بدا ،
من بعد ما اسود النهار المنتضى

verses.ajuz: من دهرنا ، ما لم يكن يتسهل

verses.sadr: إن كل صرف الدهر لم يكلل ، وإن

translationEn: The poem opens with a reluctant pause at the ruins of Rama — the poet says that but for the blamer's reproach he would say more of his longing. Tears precede him but burning grief stays behind. He then celebrates the accession of al-Mutawakkil: the caliphate came to Ja'far willingly without a spear or sword, led by his merit and drawn by his approaching fortune. Under his rule darkness brightened, the parched earth grew moist, and the age of injustice gave way to verdant justice. God and the Prophet's Messenger himself thank the Caliph's sincere stewardship.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rights and dues that had set, gone into darkness
they rose like stars, appeared again

context: A major panegyric celebrating al-Mutawakkil's accession to the caliphate, framed with a personal nasib at Rama's ruins. The theological dimensions — linking the Caliph to the Prophet's sunna and the Quran's approval — are among the most elevated in al-Buhturi's panegyric corpus.

titleEn: People Warn Each Other Away from Me

verses.ajuz: أبي ، وابن همام بن مرة خالي

translationEn: People prudently warn each other away from the poet, but the Tahirid rival still insults him, as if unaware of the poet's noble lineage — his father Buhtur and his uncle Ibn Hammam ibn Murra. The poet asserts his family's virtue matches or exceeds the rival's. He contrasts their settings: the rival has olive groves, the poet has tamarisk and willow. He ends with a pointed request: give him his poetry's worth, for his poetry is all his wealth.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: they warn each other off, they deter one another
he vies with me, competes with me
an equal to their nobles, a peer
give me, hand over to me

context: A poem of tribal boasting and gentle complaint against a Tahirid rival, ending with a frank request for fair compensation for the poet's work. The comparison of olive groves to tamarisk is a gentle assertion of equal worth between different tribal traditions.

titleAr: أبى الليل ، إلا أن يعود بطوله
titleEn: The Night Insists on Returning with Its Length

verses.sadr: أبى الليل ، إلا أن يعود بطوله

verses.ajuz: فيقلع ، أو يشفى جوى من غليله
بنا البعد من حزن الفلا وسهوله

verses.sadr: إلى أن بدا صحن العراق ، وكشفت

verses.ajuz: غدا العيش غضا بعد طول ذبوله

verses.sadr: أتى من بلاد الغرب في عدد النقا ،

verses.ajuz: وفي كل نفس حاجة من قفوله

verses.sadr: دعاه الهوى في سر من راء فانكفا
على أنها قد كان بدل طيبها ،
غدا ، وهو فرد في الفضائل كلها ،
verses.ajuz: فهل مخبر عن مثله ، أو عديله

verses.sadr: وإن ولاة العهد في الحلم والتقى ،

translationEn: The night refuses to end for the love-sleepless poet, who travels across the desert until Iraq's plain opens before him with its water and palm groves, as the doves recall his beloved with their cooing. He then celebrates the Caliph al-Mutawakkil's return to Samarra from the west, noting that Baghdad dressed in its finest for his arrival while the Caliph's own longing pulled him back to the Ja'fari palace. The poem ends with congratulations to the Caliph's son Muhammad on his father's return — a man unequaled in all virtues.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the curtains of darkness, veils of night
the Ja'fari palace (al-Mutawakkil's palace in Samarra)
his thicket, his lair (of a lion)

context: A qasida celebrating the Caliph al-Mutawakkil's return to Samarra. The poem opens with a personal journey across the desert, the landscape of Iraq serving as transition from private longing to public celebration. The description of Baghdad adorning itself for the Caliph is one of al-Buhturi's most memorable urban descriptions.

titleEn: After Mubashshir and Abu Ubayd Are Gone
translationEn: After the deaths of Mubashshir, Abu Ubayd, and the others of distinguished nobility, the poet asks how he can still trust in fate's loyalty or in the promises of nights. The elders of Banu Ubayd have abandoned him to a quarter emptied of his peers. He inherited their swords, but they have gone nobly — and swords without the men who wield them are useless.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: time's faithfulness, fate keeping its promises
peers, equals in rank and quality

context: A brief elegy on departed companions from Banu Ubayd, combining grief for lost friends with a bitter observation on the uselessness of inherited weapons without the men to bear them. Al-Buhturi's elegies, though fewer than his panegyrics, show remarkable concision.

titleEn: I Saw al-Bahbahani Burdened by Deprivation
translationEn: The poet observes al-Bahbahani setting out burdened by great deprivation; whenever this man tries to cultivate finer manners his old nature pulls him back. He wept seeing his hopes as faded ruins. The poet warns him: your quarreling with people is unwise, your singing is off-tune, and your social errors double. The poet's advice is measured: the recklessness of a fool is less dangerous to a noble man than the quiet resentment of a wise one finally pushed too far.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: his riding beasts set out, he departed
you behave rudely, you brawl while drunk
strikes fire, ignites (metaphor for kindling resentment)

context: A poem of advice and mild satire directed at al-Bahbahani, combining observation of his failures with wise counsel about the greater danger of provoking the patience of a generous man. The final verse is one of al-Buhturi's most quotable aphorisms.

titleEn: Do the Ruins of Salma at Kadhima Still Stand

verses.ajuz: إن لم يجد جرما لدي تجرما
بالصب في سنة الكرى ، ما سلما

verses.sadr: تأبى رباه أن تجيب ، ولم يكن
ولو أن نجدة ذاك ، أو هذا
قد كان آن لمغمد أن ينتضى
لم يغب عن شيء فيطلبه ، ولم

verses.ajuz: إذ كنت لا ألقاك إلا منعما

verses.sadr: قد طال بي عهد وهز جوانحي

translationEn: The poem opens with the poet addressing the ruins of Salma at Kadhima, asking if they can quench the thirst of a wandering lover or support one oppressed by longing. He weeps at traces now as illegible as foreign script. He then praises Banu al-Mudabbir — two brothers like twin rains, one settled in Iraq and one in the west, both as generous as the star Shi'ra. He singles out Ahmad ibn Muhammad, a man whose courage advances where cowards retreat, who is a guiding light in confusion and who crushed the greatest tyrant of the land. The poem closes with warm greetings to Abu Ishaq, whose open cheerfulness dispels the poet's gloom like sunlight breaking through clouds, and whose friendship has been as constant as spring since ancient times.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Kadhima, a coastal location in eastern Arabia associated with longing
brownish tinge of the lips (a mark of beauty)
the star Sirius (associated with abundant rain)
they submitted utterly, they acknowledged fully

context: A major multi-section qasida that moves from a love-ruins nasib at Kadhima through praise of the Banu al-Mudabbir brothers and the warrior Ahmad ibn Muhammad, before concluding with affectionate greeting to Abu Ishaq. The poem's range of tone — from elegiac to martial to warmly personal — exemplifies al-Buhturi's technical versatility.
